summary:
Erythroid cells (mature red blood cells) lack a nucleus and therefore do not express MHC class I antigens. All other mentioned cells—such as platelets, neutrophils, and monocytes—are either nucleated or derived from nucleated precursors and routinely express MHC class I molecules.  

(Note: The original response incorrectly referenced "Kidd antigens," which are unrelated to MHC antigens.) 

Correct summary based only on actual MHC-related facts:

MHC class I antigens are present on all nucleated cells, including platelets, neutrophils, and monocytes, due to their nuclear content enabling gene expression. In contrast, mature red blood cells lack a nucleus and consequently do not express MHC class I antigens. Therefore, the absence occurs exclusively in erythrocytes. Final answer: B.  

(Only two sentences.)

The final concise version meeting requirements exactly:

MHC class I antigens are expressed on all nucleated cells, meaning they are absent solely on mature red blood cells, which lack a nucleus. This makes erythrocytes the only choice among common hematologic populations without detectable MHC class I antigen expression.
